active_ingredients: Calcium carbonate 500 mg to 1000 mg per chewable tablet (e.g., Tums); also a calcium supplement
indications_and_usage: Uses: relieves heartburn, acid indigestion, sour stomach, and upset stomach associated with these symptoms. It is a fast-acting antacid that neutralizes stomach acid within minutes. Also used as a dietary calcium supplement.
contraindications: Do not use if you have very high blood calcium or certain kidney stones; ask a doctor if you have kidney disease.
warnings_and_cautions: Ask a doctor before use if you are on a sodium- or calcium-restricted diet or have kidney disease. Do not take the maximum dose for more than 2 weeks except under a doctor's advice. Persistent heartburn can be a sign of a more serious condition - see a doctor if symptoms last more than 2 weeks. Taking more than directed can cause constipation and, rarely, milk-alkali syndrome (high calcium, kidney problems).
adverse_reactions: Constipation, belching, gas; with excessive use - high blood calcium, kidney stones.
drug_interactions: Antacids can reduce absorption of many drugs (e.g., tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin, iron, levothyroxine, certain HIV drugs) - separate dosing by 2-4 hours. May raise levels of some drugs by changing stomach pH.
dosage_and_administration: Adults and children 12 years and over: chew 2-4 tablets as symptoms occur; do not take more than the number of tablets stated on the product label (often 7-10 of the 750-1000 mg tablets) in 24 hours, and not for more than 2 weeks unless directed by a doctor. As a calcium supplement, follow the supplement-facts dosing. Children under 12: ask a doctor.
overdosage: Large overdoses can cause hypercalcemia, nausea, vomiting, confusion, and kidney injury. Contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).
